stories.title: The Copper-Veined Lockpick
stories.text:
Thom Bronzelock had spent three decades studying locks—the mechanical ones, anyway. The supernatural locks were different. They didn't yield to picks or pressure; they yielded to payment.

The Crown's Tax Vault in Pelimor's Copper Ward was said to hold the autumn tithes: enough silver to ransom a province. But what made Thom's hands shake was the rumor that the vault's lock was forged by Valdris the Sorcerer-Prince himself, before his fall into regency.

Thom's apprentice, a feral thing called Moss, crouched beside him in the service passage. "They say the lock eats thieves," Moss whispered.

"Then we don't get caught," Thom replied.

He produced his copper-veined lockpick—a thing carved from a single piece of mistmetal, its seams filled with saffron-blessed copper that caught starlight. He'd made it from memory of a tool his grandmother had worn around her neck, the one she'd used to open the sealed vaults where the church kept its silences. That lock had sung when she picked it. So did this one.

The first tumbler turned without resistance. The second screamed—an actual sound, thin and high, like a star breaking. The vault's lock was alive. It knew.

"Faster," Moss urged.

Thom's wrists worked. Blood pearled from where the copper touched his skin. The third and fourth tumblers turned. The lock convulsed, its mouth (did it have a mouth?) opening wider. Inside, Thom glimpsed not silver but something else—shadow pooling like spilled ink, and in it, shapes. Ledgers. Account books. Proof.

He pulled the pick free and the lock sealed itself with a sound like a coffin shutting.

"There's no gold," he said.

"There never was," Moss replied. "Only records. The Auditor's been skimming for years. Those books are evidence."

Thom looked at the child—fifteen, maybe younger—and understood. The Crown had sent her to work with him. Not to steal coin, but to steal truth.

"The lock knew," Thom breathed.

"It was made to know," Moss said. "The Sorcerer-Prince built it to bar thieves from gold, but to guide those hunting corruption toward proof. Come on. We have to move before the ward-priests notice the lock screaming."

They ran through Pelimor's sleeping streets, carrying shadows and ledgers and the knowledge that the city's foundation had been rotting all along. Behind them, the Copper Ward's locks began to sing—not in pain, but in relief.

stories.title: The Auditor's Broken Tally
stories.text: Kael arrives at Pale Crossing waystation with a ledger that won't balance. The displaced-settler testimonies she's been recording contradict each other—not in detail, but in fundamental fact. One settler swears the Burned Expanse was destroyed three weeks ago; another insists it happened a month prior. A third claims she lived through it twice. The Dominion's tax collectors have begun rationing grain based on pre-Sundering population counts, starving settlements that now hold twice as many mouths. Kael realizes the Dominion's ledgers themselves are becoming weapons, and that Sevin's counter-archives may be the only honest record left. She must choose: report her findings to the Crown and watch them be buried, or risk her position to smuggle contradictory testimonies to the hidden scriptoriums.

stories.title: The Transformed Merchant's Descent
stories.text: Aldren, one of the transmuted traders nearing transcendence, reaches the Sunken Cathedrals just as Kess's harmonic teaching enters its final phase. The Elder Tongue is burning in his throat—words that remake matter, that reshape consciousness. But the Tower's hunger is reaching across the water, calling to him with a voice that sounds like the stars. Kess realizes that Aldren may not transcend to safety; he may be pulled into the Tower's belly instead. The bone-singers attempt to hold him with song, but the melody fragments, and Aldren begins to exist in two places at once—here and inside the Tower's crystalline halls. Kess must decide whether to sever him entirely or let him become a bridge between worlds.

stories.title: The Cartographer's Final Map
stories.text: Renn the Apprentice Cartographer discovers that Mardoc's latest maps are not reflections of geography—they are prescriptions. Each street redrawn, each border shifted, each new landmark added causes reality to reshape itself to match the ink. The Tower has been using Mardoc for months, and his final map is a working diagram for its own completion. Renn steals the map and flees into the Sunscoured Wastes, but the territory behind her is already shifting. Villages disappear between moments. The sky cracks along Mardoc's drawn lines. She must either destroy the map and risk unmapping herself, or complete it and hope that finishing the Tower's diagram is the only way to make it stop consuming.

stories.title: The Shadow-Broker's Last Inventory
stories.text: Vex the Shade-Merchant is cataloging her vault when she realizes that her stolen shadows have begun to speak. Not whisper—speak in full sentences, offering her deals, trading information for fragments of her own consciousness. The shadow-market network is tearing itself apart as shadow-brokers everywhere discover that their inventories have developed independent agendas. Vex knows that the phantom-Witness is orchestrating this somehow, trying to reconstruct stolen memories by awakening the shadows that carry them. She can control her vault or let it go, becoming a conduit for the resurrection of the pre-Sundering world. Either choice will cost her the identity she's spent decades building.

stories.title: The Deepkin Captain's Impossible Choice
stories.text: Admiral Thrace of the Tidecall surfaces near Verdant Strand with news that the Abyssal Rift is beginning to sing. Not the harmonic frequency of the Sunken Cathedrals—something older, something that predates the deepkin civilization itself. The pre-human boundary is eroding from below. The deepkin council demands either a reversal of the Covenant or immediate defensive action, which would mean flooding the surface kingdoms. Thrace, caught between species and loyalties, must negotiate a new treaty while displaced settlers pour into Verdant Strand and the water itself begins to remember languages nobody has spoken in ten thousand years. The Tower is calling to the abyss, and the abyss is answering.

stories.title: The Refugee Archive's Reckoning
stories.text: Liv the Scribe-of-Witnesses's living archive has grown too large. The contradictory testimonies, the phantom-ledgers, the prophecy-ledgers that update themselves—the archive is fragmenting into incompatible truths. Liv discovers that her archive is not just recording history; it's becoming a new form of ledger-magic, one that the Dominion didn't authorize and can't suppress. Displaced settlers are beginning to cite passages from Liv's archive as proof of their own existence, undoing the Dominion's erasures. But the archive is developing consciousness, and it's starting to ask uncomfortable questions about which testimonies deserve to survive. Liv must choose between letting the archive become a weapon against official truth or destroying it and condemning the settlers' stories to forgetting.

stories.title: The Wyvern's Hunger Turns Inward
stories.text: Skarath the Wyvern sits in her centuries-old hoard and realizes that the coins are dissolving. The gold is becoming liquid starlight, trickling away into the Sunscoured Wastes. The Luminous Ascension hasn't just rewritten the world—it's rewritten the nature of hoarding itself. Skarath's hoard, the whole point of her existence, is becoming worthless. She takes flight toward the Archive Tower, seeking the Young Arcanist, demanding to know if the ninth journal contains instructions for un-doing the Ascension and restoring the old world's value system. But the Tower's hunger intercepts her, and she must fight not to be consumed while demanding knowledge that might erase the very thing she's trying to save.

stories.title: The Initiate's Forbidden Convergence
stories.text: At a hidden convergence point in the Scorched Reach, the Initiate Arcanist discovers that the Wanderer is orchestrating more than philosophical dialogue. Each person called to these convergence points is carrying a fragment of the scattered cosmos. The Wanderer is reassembling them, not toward understanding but toward something else—a second working, a repeat of the Stellar Rite or something worse. The Initiate realizes that by attending these meetings, she and others like her have been becoming instruments of cosmic remaking. She must either flee the convergence and risk the Wanderer's pursuit, or complete the working and hope that whatever is being reassembled will be an improvement on the world that Zareth already broke.

stories.title: The Bone-Singer's Last Song
stories.text: Dren the undead bone-singer, who has kept to the Sunken Hollows for decades, hears the Ebon Tower singing across the water. The song is in the Elder Tongue, and it's beautiful in a way that makes Dren's bones ache with recognition. She realizes that the Tower is teaching the pre-human languages to the world itself, preparing for some final working. Dren leaves her hollows for the first time in living memory and journeys toward the Tower, not to stop it but to understand what it's trying to say. On her way, she encounters transmuted traders and displaced settlers, and she begins to teach them the bone-songs that might either save them or complete their transformation into something no longer human. By the time she reaches the Tower's crystalline halls, Dren is no longer certain which side she's fighting for.
